div.p: When I heard of the death of our beloved Brother Barnes, it would not have affected me so much, if I had the opportunity of burying him in the land of Zion.

I believe those who have buried their friends here, their condition is enviable. Look at Jacob and Joseph in Egypt, how they required their friends to bury them in the tomb of their fathers.1 See the expense which attended the embalming and the going up of the great company to the burial.2
It has always been considered a great calamity not to obtain an honorable burial: and one of the greatest curses the ancient prophets could put on any man, was that he should go without a burial.

I have said, Father, I desire to die here among the Saints. But if this is not Thy will, and I go hence and die,3 wilt Thou find some kind friend to bring my body back,4 and gather my friends who have fallen in foreign lands, and bring them up hither, that we may all lie together.

I will tell you what I want. If tomorrow I shall be called to lie in yonder tomb, in the morning of the resurrection let me strike hands5 with my father, and cry, “My father,” and he will say, “My son, my son,”6 as soon as the rock rends7 and before we come out of our graves.8

And may we contemplate these thing so? Yes, if we learn how to live and how to die. When we lie down we contemplate how we may rise in the morning; and it is pleasing for friends to lie down together, locked in the arms of love,9 to sleep and wake in each other’s embrace and renew their conversation.
div.div: The Righteous to Have Joy in the Resurrection
div.p: Would you think it strange if I relate what I have seen in vision in relation to this interesting theme? Those who have died in Jesus Christ10 may expect to enter into all that fruition of joy when they come forth,11 which they possessed or anticipated here.12

So plain was the vision, that I actually saw men, before they had ascended from the tomb, as though they were getting up slowly. They took each other by the hand and said to each other, “My father, my son, my mother, my daughter, my brother, my sister.” And when the voice calls for the dead to arise,13 suppose I am laid by the side of my father, what would be the first joy of my heart? To meet my father, my mother,
